title: Methods & Recursion
learningObjectives: Explain Java pass-by-value for primitives and reference copies for objects
Define method overloading, resolution order, and why return types cannot disambiguate
Write recursive methods with base cases, progress metrics, and stack-depth awareness
Contrast naive recursive Fibonacci with iterative O(n) approaches for production limits
Diagnose StackOverflowError versus logic bugs in recursive parsers and utilities
Use static versus instance methods appropriately in small demo programs

sections.title: Why methods, overload resolution, and recursion discipline matter
sections.content:
Methods are the contract surface of every Java service: they name behaviour, hide state transitions, and encode the invariants reviewers interrogate in pull requests. Recursion is the same idea applied with a stack of pending work—elegant for trees and divide-and-conquer, lethal when base cases are missing or depth is unbounded. When either story is misunderstood, production failures look like “mystery nulls” or sudden **StackOverflowError** spikes, but the root cause is almost always parameter passing semantics or call-depth discipline.

Java is strictly **pass-by-value**: primitives copy bits; object references copy pointer-sized values that still alias one heap object. That distinction confuses juniors because mutating fields through a copied reference looks like “pass-by-reference,” while reassigning the parameter never rewires the caller’s variable. Interviewers probe this boundary constantly because it predicts whether you can debug aliasing bugs in DTOs, collections, and array-backed buffers without folklore.

Overloading lets one method name cover multiple parameter shapes. Resolution happens at compile time using the **static** types of arguments, followed by widening, autoboxing, and varargs in a defined order. Return type alone cannot disambiguate overloads—if two methods differ only by return type, the program does not compile. Misunderstanding resolution leads to surprise calls into widening paths that allocate or lose precision silently.

Recursion requires a **base case** that terminates, a **recursive case** that moves toward it, and a story about **stack depth**. Java does not optimise general tail recursion into iteration; deep recursion exhausts the thread stack and fails with **StackOverflowError**, which is not catchable like normal exceptions in the way engineers wish. Production code often replaces deep recursion with explicit stacks or loops while keeping recursive forms for clarity on bounded depth problems.

Day six’s assignment chains pass-by-value demos, invalid overload attempts, factorial base cases, and Fibonacci recursion. Fibonacci as naïve recursion is intentionally exponential—fine for ten terms, unacceptable for large **n** without memoisation or iteration. The pedagogical point is to see the call tree, not to ship that version into latency-sensitive paths.

During **on-call**, you may not debug factorial directly, but you will debug parsers and evaluators that recurse through JSON trees, expression engines, or directory walks. The same skills apply: validate depth, guard cycles, log frame identifiers, and cap work with iterative alternatives when user input controls depth. Code review language you should adopt names **preconditions**, **postconditions**, and **progress** toward termination—terms that scale from recursion to distributed retries. This day trains you to narrate methods the way senior engineers do: signature first, invariants second, complexity third, and a concrete test or metric that proves the story. When you can explain why the caller’s `int` did not change but the array slot did, and why missing factorial base cases brick the JVM stack, you are ready for the coding rounds that follow these topics into dynamic programming and tree algorithms. When you sit with observability tools, method boundaries are where you place timers, trace spans, and argument redaction. A method that secretly recurses without logging depth becomes a black box during incidents—especially when malformed input triggers exponential recomputation. Treat every recursive helper like a tiny service: document maximum depth, fail fast on bad states, and prefer iterative equivalents when untrusted data controls the shape of the call graph. Overload mistakes show up in code review as “wrong method picked” bugs when literals widen unexpectedly or varargs compete with explicit arrays. Recursion mistakes appear as flaky tests that pass on small data but fail under production-sized trees. Learn both failure modes now so your future self can spot them in stack traces without guessing. That discipline is what interviewers mean when they ask for “production-ready” Java—not fancier syntax, but explicit reasoning about calls, stacks, and types.

sections.title: Theory and Internals — calls, frames, overloads, and recursion
sections.content:
### Plain-language overview

A **method** is a named, callable block with parameters, return type (or `void`), and thrown effects described by `throws`. **Recursion** is a method calling itself (directly or indirectly) with the expectation that a **base case** eventually stops the chain. Java evaluates arguments **before** the call, pushes a **stack frame** for each invocation, and pops it when the method completes or throws. Understanding that lifecycle is how you connect textbook recursion to **StackOverflowError** incidents.

### 1. Pass-by-value semantics

Java always passes **by value**. For `int`, the value is the bits of the integer. For `Object`, the value is the **reference**—a copy of the pointer—not the object itself. Mutating fields through that reference affects the shared object; reassigning the parameter to a new object does **not** reassign the caller’s variable. **Interview angle:** Contrast “I changed the object” with “I repointed the variable.”

### 2. Primitives versus references on the stack frame

Local primitives live in the frame as values; local object variables hold references. When you pass `int x` into a method, the callee receives an independent copy. When you pass `int[] arr`, the callee receives a copy of the reference pointing at the same array object. **Interview angle:** Draw three boxes: caller var, parameter var, heap object.

### 3. Method signatures and `static`

Instance methods receive a hidden `this` reference; `static` methods belong to the class and cannot access instance fields without an instance. Recursion works the same for both—each call still consumes a frame. **Interview angle:** Explain why `main` is `static`: JVM calls it without constructing an object first.

### 4. Overloading rules

Overloaded methods share a name but differ in **parameter count, type, or order**. The compiler picks the **most specific** match using phases: exact match, widening, autoboxing/unboxing, varargs. **Return type alone cannot disambiguate**—compilation fails if two overloads differ only by return type. **Interview angle:** Give an invalid pair `(int,int)` returning `int` vs `double`.

### 5. Widening and boxing interaction

A call `add(1,1)` might prefer `add(int,int)` over `add(double,double)` because `int` to `double` is widening but less specific than exact `int`. Boxing can interact with overloads in subtle ways—know that `null` targets reference types, not primitives. **Interview angle:** Mention varargs as “last resort” in overload resolution.

### 6. Varargs

`void log(String first, String... rest)` packages trailing arguments into a `String[]`. Varargs must be the **last** parameter. Passing an explicit array is allowed. **Interview angle:** Warn that `method(null)` can bind to a varargs parameter unexpectedly—prefer overloads or casts when ambiguous.

### 7. Recursion structure

Every recursive method needs: (1) **base case**—return without calling itself; (2) **recursive case**—calls itself with **progress** toward the base. Factorial missing `n<=0` dives negative forever. **Interview angle:** State progress metric: `n` decreases toward zero.

### 8. Call stack and StackOverflowError

Each recursive call consumes stack space for locals, parameters, and return addresses. **StackOverflowError** means depth exceeded configured stack size—often infinite or exponential recursion. It is an **Error**, not a checked `Exception`; catching it is almost always wrong. **Interview angle:** Java lacks general tail-call optimisation—iterate or use explicit `Deque` for deep structures.

### 9. Fibonacci naïve complexity

`fib(n)=fib(n-1)+fib(n-2)` without memoisation recomputes subproblems exponentially—fine for `n<40` demos, not for production `n`. **Interview angle:** Offer iterative or memoised version as “what I’d ship.”

### 10. Tail recursion concept (limits in Java)

Tail recursion places the recursive call as the last action; some languages optimise it away. **Java does not guarantee** this optimisation. **Interview angle:** Say you’d rewrite hot paths as loops.

### 11. Mutual recursion

Method `a` calls `b` calls `a`—still needs global progress and base cases, otherwise cycles. Parsers often use mutual recursion for grammar rules. **Interview angle:** Mention cycle detection for user-defined grammars.

### 12. Method overloading versus overriding

Overloading is **compile-time** selection on static types. Overriding is **runtime** dispatch on dynamic types with `virtual` methods (default for instance methods). Do not confuse the two in interview answers. **Interview angle:** `final` methods cannot be overridden.

### 13. Parameter final

`void f(final int x)` prevents reassignment of `x` inside the method—useful in lambdas and clarity, does not change pass-by-value semantics. **Interview angle:** `final` on reference parameters still allows mutating the object’s fields.

### 14. Exceptions and stack unwinding

Uncaught exceptions unwind frames until a matching handler appears; `finally` blocks still run. Recursive methods must ensure **progress** even when exceptions occur—use try/finally for resource balance when teaching, though try-with-resources is idiomatic for `AutoCloseable`. **Interview angle:** Do not recurse inside `finally` accidentally.

### 15. Testing recursive code

Use small controlled inputs, property tests on invariants (`factorial` positive matches iterative), and depth-limited inputs for tree walkers. **Interview angle:** Mention stack-safe iterative golden tests for large shapes.

### 16. Assignment tie-in

Day 6 assignment: demonstrate pass-by-value prints `1` then `99`, reject invalid overloads, fix factorial base case, implement recursive Fibonacci for first ten indices. **Interview angle:** Narrate base case + progress + complexity trade-off.

### 17. 60-second interview story

Java passes arguments by value—primitives copy bits, references copy pointers. Overloading resolves at compile time; return types cannot disambiguate. Recursion needs a base case and progress; deep recursion risks **StackOverflowError** because Java does not optimise tail recursion generally. Naïve Fibonacci is exponential—iterate or memoise for real workloads. Verify with tiny tests and explicit depth limits for parsers.

### 18. Satyverse drill

Trace `change(a)` vs `changeArr(arr)` on paper. Write invalid overload `add(int,int)` with different return types and read the compiler error. Expand factorial with `n<=0` guard and assert `factorial(5)==120`.

### 19. Pitfall rehearsal table

| Symptom | Likely cause | Fix |
|---------|--------------|-----|
| StackOverflowError | missing base / infinite mutual recursion | add guard or iterate |
| Wrong overload picked | widening/varargs surprise | cast or rename |
| Caller array unchanged? | reassigned parameter only | return new array or mutate slots |

### 20. Interview angle — closing

End method questions with **pre/postconditions** and **complexity**—interviewers reward crisp contracts.

### Stack discipline layer 1

Each activation record stores parameters, locals, and the return link for that call. When widening char->int->long->float->double interacts with boxed overloads and may allocate unexpectedly, reviewers ask for explicit casts or renamed methods to remove ambiguity. Recursive services walking JSON or XML must bound depth because **StackOverflowError** is not a graceful backpressure signal—it tears the thread down. Prefer explicit `Deque` stacks or tail-recursive-by-hand loops when depth is user-controlled. **Interview angle:** Pair any recursive algorithm with worst-case depth and a plan to iterate if depth exceeds a threshold.

### Production comparison table — recursion safety in real services
| Approach | Operational upside | Production risk |
|---|---|---|
| Guarded recursion with depth checks | Preserves readability and deterministic exits | Missed edge cases if depth contracts are undocumented |
| Iterative rewrite with explicit stack | Stable for deep inputs and easier memory profiling | Higher code complexity without shared conventions |
| Hybrid strategy (recursion for shallow, iterative for deep) | Balances clarity and resilience under mixed traffic | Incorrect threshold tuning can hide latency spikes |
**Interview angle:** You explain the trade-off as a runtime reliability choice, not a style preference.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Claiming Java passes objects by reference — symptom is failed explanation when `swap(Object a,Object b)` cannot repoint callers; detect in interviews; fix language to “pass-by-value of reference.”
Defining two overloads that differ only by return type — symptom is compile error duplicate method; detect at `javac`; fix by renaming or changing parameter lists.
Recursion without base case — symptom is `StackOverflowError` under tiny input in prod parsers; detect with stack traces repeating same method; fix with guards, iteration, or explicit `Deque`.
Using naive Fibonacci for large `n` — symptom is hours-long CPU; detect with profiler flame graphs; fix with memoisation or iterative DP.
Expecting tail-call optimisation to save deep recursion in Java — symptom is still `StackOverflowError`; detect by reading JVM spec expectations; fix by rewriting loop.
Varargs ambiguity with `null` — symptom is confusing overload binding; detect with minimal repro; fix with explicit cast or overload without varargs.
Mutating shared array through alias without documenting contract — symptom is spooky action at a distance across layers; detect in code review; fix with defensive copies when needed.
Catching `StackOverflowError` for control flow — symptom is brittle recovery and hidden corruption; detect in review; fix by preventing overflow via limits.

sections.title: Exercise — Day 6 assignment: pass-by-value, overloads, factorial base, Fibonacci
sections.problem:
Implement `arch.day6.Day6Exercise` consolidating **assignments_phase1.json** Day 6 prompts.

Requirements:
1. Print **four** lines summarising pass-by-value: primitives copy values, references copy pointers, reassignment inside callee does not affect caller’s reference variable, mutating shared object fields does.
2. Demonstrate overload resolution: implement `add(int,int)` and `add(double,double)`; print `add(1,2)` and `add(1.0,2.0)` on separate lines.
3. Implement **guarded** `factorial(int n)` with base `n<=1` returning `1`; print `factorial(5)`.
4. Implement **naïve recursive** `fibonacci(int n)` with bases `n==0 -> 0`, `n==1 -> 1`; print the **first 10** numbers (indices `0..9`) space-separated on one line prefixed exactly `First 10 Fibonacci: ` to match the assignment’s expected shape.
5. Add a comment contrasting **naïve** Fibonacci time complexity with the **iterative** `O(n)` approach you would ship for large `n`.
hints: Keep factorial multiplications in `long` if you extend tests beyond 12—`int` overflows quickly.
Use `StringBuilder` to assemble the Fibonacci line if you want to avoid string concatenation in a loop.
Never drop the base case—trace `n` down to zero manually on paper before coding.

conceptual.answer: For **Is Java pass-by-reference or pass-by-value?** in the context of **Methods & Recursion**, start from JVM internals instead of only syntax. The JVM executes operations through concrete bytecode instructions and memory models, so data representation and control decisions directly affect runtime behavior. In production, weak conceptual understanding becomes incident fuel: hidden allocations, poor branch behavior, or boundary errors increase latency, retry pressure, and GC churn. Diagnose with real tooling: use `jcmd <pid> GC.heap_info` for memory posture, `jstat -gcutil <pid> 1000 10` for GC pressure trends, and `javap -c` to inspect what bytecode is actually emitted for critical logic. Version awareness matters too: Java 8/11/17/21 differ in defaults and runtime optimizations, so migration testing must validate both correctness and performance. Keep the explanation fresher-friendly by pairing internal mechanism with one simple concrete example, but keep staff-level depth by connecting every concept to an operational consequence and an observable metric.

wrongAnswers: Java passes objects by reference, so reassignment inside a method updates the caller’s variable automatically.
Two methods may overload each other if they only differ by return type with identical parameters.
Tail recursion is always optimised away by the JVM, so deep recursion is free.
Catching `StackOverflowError` is the recommended way to cap recursion depth in business logic.
`static` instance methods can participate in runtime polymorphic overriding like instance methods.
Varargs parameters can appear anywhere in the parameter list as long as the name is descriptive.
Naive Fibonacci recursion is O(n) time because each call subtracts one.
Mutual recursion cannot cause stack overflow if each method has a base case somewhere in the project.

options.B: Choose JVM-aware behavior with observability and safe rollout
options.C: Ignore edge cases and rely on retries
options.D: Assume all Java versions behave identically

questions.explanation: Staff-level reasoning connects language behavior to JVM runtime and operational evidence. B is correct. A is wrong because syntax-only thinking misses production risk; C is wrong because retries can amplify bad logic and latency; D is wrong because version differences can impact defaults and performance. sections.title: Cheatsheet — methods and recursion
sections.content:
| Topic | Rule of thumb | Interview one-liner |
|-------|---------------|---------------------|
| Pass-by-value | Copies bits / reference | "Primitives copy; references alias the heap object." |
| Reassign param | Local only | "Cannot repoint caller vars." |
| Mutate fields | Visible to all aliases | "Shared object = shared mutation." |
| Overload | Param list differs | "Return type alone is not enough." |
| Resolution | Compile-time static types | "Pick most specific; varargs last." |
| Widening | Primitive promotion chain | "byte→short→int→long→float→double." |
| Boxing | Wrapper conversions | "Impacts overload matching." |
| Varargs | Last parameter | "Compiler packs array." |
| Recursion base | Stops calls | "Without it, stack blows." |
| Progress | Toward base | "Prove n shrinks or tokens consumed." |
| StackOverflow | Too deep | "Iterate or cap depth." |
| Tail recursion | Not optimised | "Rewrite loop in Java." |
| fib naive | Exponential time | "Memo or iterate for prod." |
| factorial | Multiply down to base | "Guard n<=1." |
| Mutual recursion | Needs progress | "Cycle risks in parsers." |
